kitchen.q1: How long does a kitchen renovation typically take?
kitchen.a1: A typical kitchen renovation takes 4-8 weeks depending on the scope. Simple updates like cabinet refacing may take 1-2 weeks, while complete gut renovations with custom cabinetry can take 8-12 weeks.
kitchen.q2: What is the average cost of a kitchen renovation in {area}?
kitchen.a2: Kitchen renovations in {area} typically range from $15,000 to $75,000+ depending on size and finishes. We provide free detailed quotes after an in-home consultation.
kitchen.q3: Do I need permits for my kitchen renovation?
kitchen.a3: Permits are required for electrical, plumbing, and structural changes. We handle all permit applications and ensure your renovation meets {area} building codes.

bathroom.q1: How long does a bathroom renovation in {area} take?
bathroom.a1: Most bathroom renovations in {area} take 2-4 weeks. A simple cosmetic update may take 1 week, while a complete renovation with layout changes can take 4-6 weeks.
bathroom.q2: Can I use my bathroom during renovation?
bathroom.a2: Typically no, but we work efficiently to minimize disruption. If you have multiple bathrooms, we can help you plan around the renovation schedule.
bathroom.q3: What waterproofing do you use for bathrooms?
bathroom.a3: We use industry-leading waterproofing systems including Schluter and RedGard to ensure your bathroom is properly protected against moisture damage.

whole-house.q1: Can I live in my home during a whole house renovation in {area}?
whole-house.a1: It depends on the scope. For major renovations in {area}, we often recommend temporary relocation for safety and efficiency. We can help coordinate phased work if staying is preferred.
whole-house.q2: How do you manage a whole house renovation project?
whole-house.a2: We assign a dedicated project manager who coordinates all trades, maintains the schedule, and provides regular updates. You have a single point of contact throughout.
whole-house.q3: Do I need permits for a whole house renovation in {area}?
whole-house.a3: Yes, whole house renovations typically require multiple permits for structural, electrical, and plumbing work. We handle all {area} permit applications and inspections.

basement.q1: Do I need a permit to finish my basement in {area}?
basement.a1: Yes, basement finishing requires building permits in {area}. We handle all permit applications and inspections as part of our service.
basement.q2: How do you handle basement moisture issues?
basement.a2: We assess moisture levels and implement appropriate solutions including drainage improvements, vapor barriers, and proper insulation before finishing.
basement.q3: Can I add a bathroom to my basement?
basement.a3: Yes, we can add bathrooms to basements. If below the sewer line, we install sewage ejector systems. All plumbing meets {area} building codes.

cabinet.q1: What is the difference between refacing and replacing cabinets?
cabinet.a1: Refacing keeps your existing cabinet boxes and replaces doors, drawer fronts, and hardware. It costs 40-50% less than full replacement and takes less time.
cabinet.q2: How long does cabinet refacing take in {area}?
cabinet.a2: Cabinet refacing in {area} typically takes 3-5 days for an average kitchen. Your kitchen remains functional throughout most of the process.
cabinet.a3: Most solid wood and plywood cabinet boxes can be refaced if they are structurally sound. We assess your cabinets during a free in-home consultation in {area}.

commercial.q1: Do you work during business hours?
commercial.a1: We offer flexible scheduling including evenings and weekends to minimize disruption to your business operations. We can phase work to keep parts of your space operational.
commercial.q2: Are you experienced with {area} commercial building codes?
commercial.a2: Yes, our team is experienced with {area} commercial building codes, accessibility requirements, and fire safety regulations. We ensure full compliance with all commercial standards.
commercial.q3: Can you handle tenant improvements in {area}?
commercial.a3: Yes, we specialize in tenant improvements (TI) for offices, retail spaces, and restaurants in {area}. We work with landlords and property managers on approval processes.

realtor.q1: What is a realtor consultation in {area}?
realtor.a1: We meet your realtor (or you and your realtor together) on-site in {area} to assess what renovation work would maximize the resale value of the property. We give an honest scope-and-budget recommendation — not every property needs a full reno to sell well.
realtor.q2: How much does a pre-listing renovation in {area} cost?
realtor.a2: Pre-listing renovations in {area} typically run $3,000 (paint + minor repairs) to $40,000 (kitchen + bathroom refresh). We focus on highest-ROI items: paint, flooring, kitchen face-lift, bathroom vanity. We provide a written quote with timeline.
realtor.q3: How fast can you finish a pre-listing renovation in {area}?
realtor.a3: Most pre-listing renovations in {area} complete in 1-3 weeks: 1 week for paint-only refreshes, 2-3 weeks for kitchen/bathroom face-lifts. We coordinate with your realtor's listing timeline so the property is staging-ready on schedule.

poly-b-replacement.q1: How do I know if I have Poly-B plumbing?
poly-b-replacement.a1: Poly-B (polybutylene) was used in Vancouver-area homes built between 1985 and 1997. The pipes are usually grey or beige plastic, branded with markings like "PB-2110" or "QEST". If your home is from that era and has plastic supply lines, there is a good chance some or all of the plumbing is Poly-B.
poly-b-replacement.q2: Will my insurance cover Poly-B replacement in {area}?
poly-b-replacement.a2: Most BC insurers will not pay to replace functioning Poly-B, but they will deny renewal or hike premiums until you replace it. We provide insurer-ready documentation after the {area} project completes — pressure test results, BC permit, PEX warranty — so you can return to your insurer for renewal at standard rates.
poly-b-replacement.q3: Can I stay in my home during a Poly-B re-pipe?
poly-b-replacement.a3: Yes. We re-pipe section by section so you have water restored at the end of each working day. Plan for short shut-offs (2-4 hours) when each fixture group is connected over to PEX. We coordinate timing with you in advance.

heat-pump-hvac.q1: What heat pump rebates are available in BC right now?
heat-pump-hvac.a1: BC Hydro offers up to $3,000 for heat pump installations, the federal Greener Homes Grant adds up to $5,000, and income-qualified low-income households can stack an additional $3,000 BC Hydro top-up — combined potential up to $11,000. Eligibility depends on home energy audit results and income. We help with the paperwork.
heat-pump-hvac.q2: Will a heat pump in {area} actually keep up in winter?
heat-pump-hvac.a2: Modern cold-climate heat pumps maintain full heating capacity down to -25°C — well below {area} winter lows. We size the system based on your home's heat loss calculation, not just square footage. For homes that lose heat fast (poor insulation, lots of glazing), we add backup heat for the coldest 5-10 days per year.
heat-pump-hvac.q3: Can I keep my gas furnace as backup?
heat-pump-hvac.a3: Yes — this is called a dual-fuel or hybrid setup. The heat pump runs as the primary heat source and the gas furnace kicks in only on the coldest days. It costs more upfront but avoids the carbon-tax penalty on most heating days while keeping a backup. We recommend dual-fuel for homes that already have a working gas furnace.

accessible-bathroom.q1: How much does an accessible bathroom renovation cost?
accessible-bathroom.a1: Costs depend on scope: $3,000-$8,000 for safety basics (grab bars, comfort-height toilet, slip-resistant flooring), $10,000-$25,000 for a tub-to-walk-in-shower conversion, and $35,000-$60,000+ for a full wheelchair-accessible ensuite with curbless shower and roll-under vanity. We provide a detailed quote after assessing the existing space.
accessible-bathroom.q2: Are accessible bathroom renovations in {area} eligible for grants?
accessible-bathroom.a2: BC's Home Adaptation for Independence (HAFI) program covers up to $20,000 for income-qualified seniors in {area}. Veterans Affairs Canada has separate accessibility funding. We help with grant applications and provide the contractor documentation needed.
accessible-bathroom.q3: Can you work with our occupational therapist?
accessible-bathroom.a3: Yes — we routinely coordinate with occupational therapists when one is involved. They specify the clearances and equipment needed; we translate that into a build plan, BC Building Code compliance, and CSA B651 accessibility standard implementation.

critical-load-panel.q1: Do I need a critical load panel for a backup generator?
critical-load-panel.a1: Yes — a critical load (sub-)panel separates your essential circuits from the rest of the house so the generator can power just those circuits without trying to back up the entire load. Without it, you risk overloading the generator on the first outage. The same applies to battery backup systems like Tesla Powerwall.
critical-load-panel.q2: Can I add a critical load panel without upgrading my main panel in {area}?
critical-load-panel.a2: Often yes, if your main panel has spare breaker positions and total load capacity. For pre-2000 {area} homes with 100A service that already run heat pumps, EV chargers, or large appliances, a 200A main panel upgrade is usually required first. We assess existing capacity during the on-site visit.
critical-load-panel.q3: How long does a critical load panel installation take?
critical-load-panel.a3: A standalone sub-panel installation takes 1 day. A combined main panel upgrade (100A→200A) plus sub-panel takes 2-3 days, including BC Technical Safety Authority inspection. We coordinate the temporary power shut-off with you (typically 4-6 hours during the swap).
